title: Myrobalanus melanocarpa
taxonomy: Myrobalanus melanocarpa is classified within the family Compositae, which is a large and diverse family of flowering plants commonly known as asterids. The genus Myrobalanus is part of the tribe Heliantheae, which includes various sunflower and daisy-like species. Within this tribe, Myrobalanus melanocarpa is a unique species, although more information is needed for a precise classification. Further research is required to confirm its exact classification. Myrobalanus melanocarpa appears to be a member of the asterid clade, but the exact classification within this group is unclear.
uses: Myrobalanus melanocarpa, also known as the black myrobalan, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its extracts have been found to possess antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties, which may contribute to its potential health benefits. In traditional medicine, the fruit, bark, and leaves of the plant are used to treat conditions such as fever, diarrhea, and skin infections. The plant's extracts may also have anti-cancer properties and be used to treat conditions such as arthritis and respiratory infections. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of the plant's medicinal uses.

parts: Myrobalanus melanocarpa, also known as the Himalayan cherry, has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits. side_effects: Myrobalanus melanocarpa, also known as black myrobalan, is used in traditional medicine, but its side effects are not well-documented. However, it is known to cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals. Its use can also lead to gastrointestinal problems, such as nausea and diarrhea, due to its high tannin content. In rare cases, consuming large quantities of black myrobalan may interact with certain medications and exacerbate underlying health conditions.
